violation_indicators: Steps or stairs at entrance with NO adjacent ramp visible
Elevation change greater than 1/2 inch with only stairs as access
Entrance accessible only by climbing steps — no alternative path
Portable/temporary ramp that is clearly too steep or narrow
compliant_indicators: Concrete or metal ramp alongside or instead of stairs
Sloped walkway at grade level (no steps needed)
Ground-level entrance with no elevation change
Ramp with visible handrails on both sides and landing areas
false_positives_to_avoid: Do NOT flag if a ramp is visible even if it's on the side of the building
Do NOT flag if the entrance is at grade level (no elevation change)
Do NOT flag a slight slope that is clearly less than 1:20 — that's not a ramp situation
A loading dock ramp is not an accessible entrance ramp
missing_ramp.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: You see 2-3 concrete steps leading to a door with no ramp anywhere in the image. COMPLIANT: You see a sloped surface with handrails leading to the same level as the door, or the entrance is flush with the sidewalk.

violation_indicators: Ramp with rise greater than 6 inches that has NO handrails
Ramp with handrail on only one side (both sides required)
Stairs (3+ risers) without continuous handrails
Handrail that stops short — doesn't extend 12 inches beyond top/bottom
compliant_indicators: Handrails visible on BOTH sides of ramp or stairs
Handrails that are continuous and appear to be 34-38 inches high
Handrails with visible extensions at top and bottom of run
Grab-style round or oval rail mounted at consistent height
false_positives_to_avoid: Short ramps (rise under 6 inches) do not require handrails
Decorative railings that ARE functional handrails count as compliant
Do NOT require handrails on a ramp with less than 6 inches of rise
missing_handrail.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A ramp going up 2+ feet with bare walls or no railings on either side. COMPLIANT: Metal or wood rails running the full length of the ramp on both sides, with visible curved extensions at the top and bottom.

violation_indicators: Door opening that appears significantly narrower than a standard wheelchair (26 inches)
Double doors where only one leaf opens and the single leaf is narrow
Door frame that visually restricts the clear opening below 32 inches
Visible obstructions reducing effective door width (door stops, hardware)
compliant_indicators: Standard commercial door (typically 36 inches wide — clearly wider than a person's shoulders)
Double doors that both open to provide wide passage
Automatic sliding doors with wide opening
Door opening that could clearly fit a wheelchair with room to spare
false_positives_to_avoid: Standard commercial doors (36 inches) are almost always compliant — do NOT flag unless clearly narrow
Perspective distortion can make doors look narrower than they are
Interior closet/utility doors have different requirements than entrance doors
If a person is walking through and the door looks normal width, it's likely compliant
narrow_doorway.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A visibly narrow doorframe where you can tell a wheelchair would not fit through — the opening looks barely wider than a person. COMPLIANT: A standard or wide commercial door where there's clearly space for a wheelchair.

violation_indicators: Spherical/round knob that requires grasping and twisting to operate
Knob-style handle (ball shape) on any door along an accessible route
Small round knob requiring pinch grip
compliant_indicators: Lever-style handle (L-shaped or J-shaped)
Push bar / panic bar hardware
Pull handle (D-shaped or U-shaped)
Automatic door opener button/sensor
Push plate (no grasping needed)
false_positives_to_avoid: Lever handles that are slightly rounded are still compliant — only flag actual spherical knobs
Thumb-turn deadbolts above the main lever handle are acceptable
Gate latches that operate with a push are not round knobs
round_door_knob.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A shiny round ball-shaped knob on the door — you'd need to grip and twist it. COMPLIANT: An L-shaped lever that you push down, or a horizontal bar you push.

violation_indicators: Parking space with blue paint/striping but NO vertical sign
Sign present but mounted too low (below 60 inches federal / 80 inches CA)
Faded or unreadable parking sign
No International Symbol of Accessibility (ISA — wheelchair icon) on sign
compliant_indicators: Vertical sign with ISA (wheelchair) symbol clearly visible
Sign mounted on pole at proper height (head height or above)
In California: sign showing fine amount ($250+) and tow-away warning
Van-accessible sign where space has extra-wide aisle
false_positives_to_avoid: The ISA painted ON THE GROUND is supplemental — the vertical sign is what matters
A sign that is partially obscured by a tree but still present is not 'missing'
Loading zones are not accessible parking spaces
missing_accessible_parking_sign.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: Blue-painted parking space with striping but no sign post visible, or a sign that's at waist height. COMPLIANT: Blue space with a tall sign post showing the wheelchair symbol clearly visible above car rooflines.

violation_indicators: Accessible parking space with no visible access aisle markings
Faded striping that is barely visible
Access aisle that appears narrower than 60 inches
No blue paint border around the accessible space
Cross-hatching missing from the access aisle
compliant_indicators: Clear blue-painted border around the space
Diagonal cross-hatching in the access aisle (loading zone)
ISA symbol painted on the pavement within the space
Wide access aisle (at least 5 feet) with clear markings
false_positives_to_avoid: Wet or shadowed pavement can obscure existing striping — look carefully
Some jurisdictions use white striping instead of blue — still compliant
Recently repaved lots may have temporary markings
missing_parking_striping.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A parking space near the entrance with a sign but no painted lines, no cross-hatching, or completely faded markings. COMPLIANT: Bright blue border lines with diagonal stripes in the adjacent aisle and a wheelchair symbol painted on the pavement.

violation_indicators: Sidewalk meeting street/parking with a full vertical curb — no ramp
Accessible route that dead-ends at a curb with no transition
Crosswalk landing that has no curb ramp
Parking lot accessible aisle that has a curb without a ramp to the sidewalk
compliant_indicators: Sloped ramp cut into the curb at crosswalks and transitions
Detectable warning surface (truncated domes) at the curb ramp base
Flared sides on curb ramps
Flush transition between parking lot and sidewalk
false_positives_to_avoid: A driveway apron that slopes to street level IS a curb transition — don't flag it
If the sidewalk and parking lot are at the same level, no curb cut is needed
Curb ramps may be located nearby but out of frame
missing_curb_cut.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A straight vertical curb (4+ inches) between a parking space/crosswalk and the sidewalk with no ramp. COMPLIANT: A sloped cut in the curb with textured bumps (truncated domes) at the bottom.

violation_indicators: Room identification signs with only printed/visual text — no raised characters
No Braille visible on permanent room signs
Signs mounted in wrong location (not on latch side of door)
Restroom doors with only visual gender icons (no tactile/Braille)
compliant_indicators: Signs with visibly raised characters (you can see shadow/depth)
Braille dots visible below text on room signs
Signs mounted on the wall adjacent to the door (latch side), about shoulder height
In California: geometric shapes (triangle=men, circle=women) on restroom signs
false_positives_to_avoid: You may not be able to see raised characters or Braille from a photo taken at distance
Overhead/hanging signs do not require tactile features — only permanent room ID signs do
Directional signs (arrows, maps) have different requirements than room signs
If you cannot clearly see the sign surface, note it needs physical verification
missing_tactile_signage.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A flat printed sign on a door reading 'RESTROOM' with no visible raised letters or Braille. COMPLIANT: A sign next to the door with characters that cast slight shadows (raised), small Braille dots below, at about 5 feet height.

violation_indicators: Toilet visible with bare walls — no bars mounted on side or rear wall
Only a toilet paper holder near the toilet (not a grab bar)
Single grab bar when two are required (side AND rear)
Grab bar that appears too high or too low (should be 33-36 inches)
compliant_indicators: Horizontal bar on the side wall next to the toilet (42+ inches long)
Horizontal bar on the rear wall behind the toilet (36+ inches long)
Bars that appear to be stainless steel/metal and securely mounted
Bars at approximately waist height when seated
false_positives_to_avoid: Towel bars are NOT grab bars — they are thinner and not rated for weight
This violation can only be confirmed in restroom photos — don't guess from exterior shots
A shelf or counter near the toilet is not a grab bar substitute
missing_grab_bars.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A toilet against bare drywall with nothing to grab onto. COMPLIANT: Sturdy metal bars mounted horizontally on the wall beside and behind the toilet.

violation_indicators: Furniture, displays, or merchandise blocking a walkway to under 36 inches
A-frame signs, planters, or bike racks in the middle of a sidewalk path
Parked vehicles blocking a curb ramp or access aisle
Construction barriers with no alternative accessible path marked
Overgrown vegetation narrowing a walkway
compliant_indicators: Clear, unobstructed path at least 36 inches wide
Outdoor dining areas that leave a clear pedestrian path
Objects positioned to the side, not blocking the walking surface
Construction zones with clearly marked accessible detour
false_positives_to_avoid: People standing in a walkway are temporary — not a violation
A path that appears narrow due to photo angle may actually be wide enough
Objects placed off to the side of the path are not blocking it
Bollards spaced 36+ inches apart are not obstructions
blocked_accessible_route.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A sandwich board sign, planter box, and outdoor table creating a gap too narrow for a wheelchair in the middle of the sidewalk. COMPLIANT: The sidewalk has objects pushed to the building side with a clear 4+ foot walking lane.

violation_indicators: ALL visible entrances have steps with no ramps
Only entrance is through a heavy door at the top of stairs
No ISA signage directing to an accessible entrance
Building with only a raised entrance and no grade-level alternative
compliant_indicators: At least one entrance at grade level or with a ramp
ISA directional sign pointing to an accessible entrance
Automatic door opener at an accessible entrance
Multiple entrances visible with at least one accessible
false_positives_to_avoid: The accessible entrance may be on a different side of the building — if you only see one facade, note this
A side entrance with a ramp counts as an accessible entrance
If you see an ISA directional sign, there IS an accessible entrance somewhere
no_accessible_entrance.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: The front of a building with steps at every door and no ramp, no ISA signs, no alternative path visible. COMPLIANT: At least one doorway reachable without climbing steps, or an ISA sign pointing around the corner.

violation_indicators: Single step (over 1/2 inch) at the entrance threshold with no ramp
Two or three steps at an entrance with no alternative access
A lip or raised threshold clearly higher than 1/2 inch
compliant_indicators: Flush/level entrance — no step at all
Threshold that is clearly very low (1/2 inch or less)
Entrance with a small ramp or beveled threshold
Automatic doors with flush floor transition
false_positives_to_avoid: Very small thresholds (under 1/2 inch) are compliant — don't flag tiny lips
A single step that has a portable ramp is partially addressed
Weather stripping creating a small bump is not the same as a step
step_only_entrance.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A clear step-up (2+ inches) to get through the front door with no ramp. COMPLIANT: The door threshold is flush with the ground or has a very slight beveled transition.

violation_indicators: Wall-mounted objects (fire extinguishers, shelves, signs) sticking out more than 4 inches between 27-80 inches height
Overhead signs with brackets protruding into the walking path
Open stairways with no cane-detectable barrier underneath
Wall-mounted displays or equipment in a corridor
compliant_indicators: Recessed objects (in alcoves or wall cutouts)
Objects mounted above 80 inches (overhead) or below 27 inches (cane-detectable)
Cane-detectable barriers placed below protruding objects
Objects flush with or close to the wall surface
false_positives_to_avoid: Objects below 27 inches from the floor can protrude any amount
Objects above 80 inches are not restricted
Columns and permanent building elements are not protruding objects
You often cannot accurately judge protrusion depth from a photo — note it needs measurement
protruding_objects.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A fire extinguisher cabinet sticking out 6 inches from the wall at about 4 feet height in a hallway. COMPLIANT: The same cabinet recessed into the wall so it's flush with the surface.

violation_indicators: Service counter that is uniformly high (above 36 inches) with no lowered section
Reception desk with no portion low enough for wheelchair approach
All counter surfaces at standing height only
compliant_indicators: A lowered section of counter (36 inches or below) at least 36 inches long
A separate accessible counter or service window at lower height
A fold-down or pull-out shelf at accessible height
Clipboard/writing surface offered as alternative
false_positives_to_avoid: Some counters have a lowered section that's not visible from all angles
A cashier window/opening that is at accessible height counts
If you can't see the full counter, note it needs physical verification
inaccessible_counter.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A long, uniformly high counter (chest height) with no dip or lowered section anywhere along it. COMPLIANT: A counter with a clearly visible lowered section (about elbow height when seated) with space underneath for wheelchair knees.

violation_indicators: Building with accessible entrance on a different side but no signs directing to it
Complex with multiple buildings and no ISA wayfinding signs
Accessible features (elevator, restroom) with no directional signs
compliant_indicators: ISA (wheelchair) symbol on directional signs at decision points
Clear arrows pointing to accessible features
Consistent wayfinding signage throughout the facility
false_positives_to_avoid: If all entrances are accessible, directional signage to an accessible entrance is not required
Interior wayfinding may not be visible from exterior photos
Small businesses with simple layouts may not need extensive wayfinding
missing_directional_signage.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A building where the accessible entrance is around the corner but the front has no sign indicating that. COMPLIANT: A sign with a wheelchair symbol and arrow saying 'Accessible Entrance →' visible from the main approach.

violation_indicators: Ramp that appears very steep — visually steeper than a standard driveway
Short ramp covering a large elevation change (rise over 30 inches)
Ramp where water would run down quickly (indicating steep grade)
Person leaning forward significantly while going up the ramp
compliant_indicators: Ramp with a gentle, gradual slope (1:12 or less — barely noticeable)
Long ramp run for a modest height change
Ramp with intermediate landings (flat areas) breaking up the slope
Ramp that appears about as steep as a standard sidewalk cross-slope
false_positives_to_avoid: Camera angle can make ramps look steeper than they are — note this uncertainty
Driveways and vehicle ramps have different slope requirements
A ramp that looks steep but is actually 1:12 should not be flagged without measurement
Always note that slope requires physical measurement to confirm
steep_ramp.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A ramp that rises sharply — it looks like you'd have difficulty pushing a wheelchair up it. The rise covers maybe 3 feet in a very short horizontal distance. COMPLIANT: A long, gentle ramp where the slope is barely visible — it takes many feet of ramp to gain a small amount of height.

violation_indicators: Curb ramp transition to street with a smooth surface — no truncated domes
Transit platform edge with no tactile warning strip
Flush pedestrian/vehicle boundary with no textural change
compliant_indicators: Truncated dome surface (bumpy texture) at curb ramp base
Yellow truncated domes in California (other colors elsewhere)
Tactile strip along transit platform edges
Cast-in-place or surface-applied dome tiles at transitions
false_positives_to_avoid: Textured concrete that is not truncated domes (e.g., broom finish) is not the same
Detectable warnings are only required at specific locations — not every sidewalk edge
If you can't see the ground surface clearly, note it needs physical verification
missing_detectable_warnings.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A curb ramp that slopes smoothly to the street with a plain concrete surface — no bumps or texture change at the bottom. COMPLIANT: Bright yellow (in CA) bumpy dome texture covering the bottom portion of the curb ramp where it meets the street.

violation_indicators: Restroom door that appears too narrow for wheelchair entry
No ISA symbol on or near the restroom
Restroom with only a round knob on the door
Visible cramped interior that would not allow wheelchair turning
compliant_indicators: Wide restroom door (36+ inches) with lever handle
ISA symbol on the restroom sign
Spacious interior visible through open door
Grab bars visible inside
false_positives_to_avoid: Cannot fully assess restroom accessibility from exterior photo alone
A restroom that looks small from outside may have adequate turning space inside
Always note that interior assessment requires physical inspection
inaccessible_restroom.visual_comparison: VIOLATION: A narrow restroom door with a round knob and no accessibility signage. COMPLIANT: A wide door with lever handle, ISA sign, and visible interior space.

_meta.usage: This reference guide is injected into Gemini prompts to improve violation detection accuracy. Each entry describes what violations and compliance look like visually, plus common false positives to avoid.
important_principles: Only flag violations you can VISUALLY CONFIRM in the image
When uncertain, mark as low confidence and note it needs physical verification
Camera angle and distance can distort perception — account for this
A feature being out of frame does not mean it's missing — be conservative
Always prefer false negatives (missing a violation) over false positives (flagging something compliant)
Measurements from photos are estimates only — note when physical measurement is needed
